editorialSummary.text: Hip restaurant with an industrial decor & a menu of local ingredients cooked to order.

text.text:
It was really a local restaurant,the day we visit was full of local, and saw a Czech’ ball game competition by chance, the atmosphere was great✨ And the staff are so nice and helpful!!

We have ordered chicken breast schnitzel, pork cutlet schnitzel, roasted beef with bread dumplings, and beef tartare.

To be honest, i know schnitzel is the traditional dishes,but it’s just not my type, the meat is not juicy enough, thin and dry actually. Fortunately, it has thin fried pork skin, and the seasoning is not so heavy, not too bad actually if that was the traditional cooking method haha.

The roasted beef with bread dumplings is same same, not my type. The portions are very small and the bread dumplings are…. i was the one love potatoes most, but i can’t understand the bread dumplings…🥲

Me and my friends love the beef tartare most, the  toast is buttery, super match with the raw beef, the beef was mix with onions, refresh the grease from meat. Recommend!!

text.text:
We visited Lokál in Prague for a classic Czech pub experience and it did not disappoint. The atmosphere was lively, local, and super authentic — exactly what you want when trying traditional Czech food.
We ordered the dumplings and schnitzel, and both were delicious. The dumplings were soft and filling, perfect with the sauce, and the schnitzel was crispy on the outside and tender inside. Portions were generous and felt like real comfort food.
The highlight for me was the raspberry beer — refreshing, slightly sweet, and a fun twist from the usual beer options.
Service was quick and efficient, and overall it was a great place to enjoy Czech food in a casual, local setting. Would definitely come back!
Highly recommended if you’re in Prague and want a true local pub + food experience.

text.text:
A solid place for a traditional meal, though some flavors are an acquired taste. And very quick service

• Strong Beef Soup: Very original taste. Add a bit of salt, pepper, and lemon to really bring out the flavors.
• Braised Beef (Svíčková): The meat and main sauce were delicious, though the traditional cranberry topping wasn't quite my taste.
• Desserts: I tried the caramel glazed profiterole. It was okay, but if you're not a dessert person, you can safely skip this part of the menu.
• Coffee: I tried the Czech-style Turkish coffee. A friendly note to the staff: Turkish coffee should always be served with a glass of water, even in Prague! ;)

I managed to find a table for Sunday at 16:00 without any issues; there were plenty of available spots at that time.

text.text:
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Exceptional Flavors in the Heart of Prague
This place is a massive, high-energy hub that lives up to its popularity. Despite not having a reservation, the wait was surprisingly short—only about 5 minutes before we were seated.
The culinary experience was the highlight:
• Strawberry Beef: A unique and sophisticated dish that was executed perfectly.
• Butcher’s Goulash: Rich, hearty, and deeply flavorful.
• The Liver: A delightful surprise! We didn't expect to find such high-quality liver in Prague, but it was a standout.
The service is remarkably attentive; the staff constantly checks in, offering extra sauces to complement the meal.
Pro Tip: The atmosphere is very lively and can get quite noisy, so be prepared for a high-decibel dining experience.
Value: Prices are very reasonable (average range) for the quality provided. While a 10% tip is suggested/expected, it remains at your discretion. Highly recommended for foodies visiting the city!

text.text: Wow what an experience! Every dish was presented beautifully and dramatically, our waiter really had a flair for performing. We received endless amuse bouches and ended up eating so much food we could barely finish the many desserts and had to basically roll home. The food was an extremely innovative take on Czech cuisine, although I did not enjoy a few dishes overall the experience was amazing. Could not fault how smoothly the dinner went and the flawless execution. Also my partner had the vegetarian menu and it was just as delicious and thoughtful as the regular menu. One thing I disliked was how most of the dishes were extremely salty but overall they spaced out the dishes well enough that it did not become overwhelming

text.text:
Overall it was an amazing experience and we enjoyed it very much, but I do have some inputs…

The food was incredible - well developed flavors, top of the line techniques, fresh and unique - but at times overwhelming to the pallet and with very few breaks between dishes. Could have desperately used a pallet cleanser. Some dishes were too salty - not at an “I am returning this” level, but at a mildly unpleasant level. The Duck dish was unbalanced.
To us personally it was a bit too much. For future experiences we would prefer places that also serve a la carte.

What I’ve learned over the years is that Michelin can come in many shapes and forms and this one was the most like the movies - super fancy, everything timed to perfection, synchronized professional staff, only an excessive tasting menu and an impressively quite open kitchen. But we always go on very special occasions and always get asked if we are celebrating something and get some reaction and noting. Nothing here. Which was a bit disappointing when you chose them for a 20 year anniversary… it was a 2.5 hr meal with 90% of that time needed to describe the dishes, so I guess they didn’t have time. In other words - it was a bit too uptight for me ;)

text.text:
We enjoyed a six-course degustation with matching wines at Field, and it was an exceptional experience from start to finish. The seasonal menu, focused on the best of Czech ingredients, showcased incredible creativity and balance.

Watching the sauce flambéed tableside added a theatrical touch that made the meal even more memorable. My favourite dish was the fallow deer with mushroom, truffle, and chokeberry — rich, earthy, and perfectly executed.

The service was outstanding: professional, warm, and perfectly paced. Every detail felt carefully considered without being pretentious.

Overall, Field delivers a refined yet grounded Michelin-star experience that celebrates local flavours in a contemporary way. Highly recommended.

text.text:
Celebrated my birthday at La Degustation Bohême Bourgeoise, and the entire evening was nothing short of exceptional. From the moment we arrived, the service was warm, precise, and genuinely attentive without ever feeling intrusive. The staff made the occasion feel truly special, which elevated the experience beyond just dining.

The tasting menu was a masterclass in creativity and balance. Each course felt thoughtfully composed, highlighting humble Central European ingredients while transforming them into something elegant and surprising. The progression of flavors was flawless, from the delicate interplay of cabbage, horseradish, and parsley root, to the beautifully refined trout with beurre blanc and apple. The catfish course was deeply comforting yet sophisticated, while the beef with beer and pea showcased remarkable depth and technique. The dessert, pairing potato with sour cherry and gingerbread, was both unexpected and memorable.

What stands out most is the restaurant’s ability to combine technical excellence with emotional warmth. This was not just a meal, but a carefully orchestrated experience. For a birthday celebration, I could not have chosen a better place, the team went above and beyond to make the evening unforgettable.

A truly remarkable dining experience that I will remember for a long time.

text.text:
28th Dec 25
La Degustation remains the definitive benchmark for modern Czech gastronomy. Chefs demonstrated technical precision, delivering delicious, unexpected flavor combinations—featuring regional ingredients with a finesse I have never encountered elsewhere.

The open kitchen allowed me to witness the brigade’s quiet, symphonic efficiency firsthand.

The wine pairings were absolutely on point, curated with a deep understanding of each dish's nuances.

Most importantly, the hospitality feels entirely genuine. The team provides seamless, attentive service that never feels robotic—a rare and refreshing quality at this level of fine dining.

I left feeling very satisfied with the entire experience. A highly recommended pilgrimage for any serious epicurean in Prague.

text.text:
Friendly service, however, only the trout surprised me.

The service was friendly and attentive—the staff patiently answered my questions and asked for my opinion throughout the meal, which I appreciated. But it felt rushed, with staff trying to clear my plate while I was still eating.

Regarding the food, only the trout truly impressed me. The sauce was excellent, and the combination of the crispy bread with the tender, fresh trout was genuinely delicious and became the highlight of the meal.

Other dishes were merely satisfactory and some were even disappointing.

For example, the glass of chicken broth served as an appetizer tasted very bitter and tastes disgusting . This is very confusing and disappointing.

The lemonade I ordered, doesn’t taste like lemonade at all.

The main dish, grilled beef with a bun, was also disappointing—the beef tasted very salty, and the bun was just a regular mini bun ( with regular pickles)with nothing special. I don’t even know why it was included in the menu. Considering the hype and price, better alternatives should have been used. The waiter called it a “dumpling,” but dumplings usually have fillings inside—this was simply plain dough.( this feels like a joke and ruined it all)

The desserts were extravagantly sweet and has nothing special, so I left them unfinished.

Overall, though the Trout dish was great, the others were mediocre and some even disappointing. Considering the price (€150 / 260 AUD) and the Michelin-star expectations, this experience did not feel worth it. You can even call it a ripoff.

text.text:
Bored to death.

Overall ambience was bland and tasteless. Except perhaps for the "bone broth" chandelier's backstory.

The amuse bouche were passable, nothing extraordinary. The lick plate was feral. The mains were not phenomenal. And cutting that long marshmallow was just very gimmicky. Not quite sure where the Michelin star quality was.

Also, if they want to carry on with an open kitchen, at least acknowledge the customers that are just an inch away. Or better yet, put up a full floor-to-ceiling glass wall so we can enjoy all the cooking without having to hear the background chatter or endure pretending we don't exist.

The little liar of a sommelier said there was no rosé available yet on the website, the drinks menu was there and I wasn't even given one to choose. It's one of those annoying new approach of "let me be your wine menu". The wine recommendation was absolutely poor.

text.text:
Eska Restaurant and Bakery absolutely nailed it. We ordered the Breakfast Karlín (a must), which comes with their phenomenal bread, juicy sausage, silky white butter beans in miso, and a sweet vanilla curd with fruit. Every element was balanced and deeply flavorful. We also had the poached egg with truffles (another must-have): rich, aromatic, and perfectly cooked.

As a black coffee lover, the brewed coffee here is among the best I’ve had in Europe—clean, complex, and with the right level of acidity. If you enjoy black coffee, order the brewed coffee. The fermented hibiscus lemonade was bright and refreshing. And that bread… exceptional crumb and the best crust. When the “basics” like bread and coffee are this good, you know everything else will shine.

Service is friendly and efficient. It’s order at the counter, then they bring everything to your table, smooth and quick even when busy. The space is lively and modern with a view of the bakery; there’s indoor and outdoor seating.

Bottom line: Destination-worthy breakfast/brunch spot where craft and flavor meet. Don’t miss the Breakfast Karlín, truffled poached egg, and brewed black coffee.

text.text: The best breakfast destination we visited in Prague was @eska_pekarna. Every dish is incredibly fresh and delicious. Their motto is simple: Processing fresh, high-quality produce in the most ideal way. They have an open kitchen where you can see almost all of the production. I also recommend their coffee options. The service staff is friendly and very prompt. The only downside is that it's a bit far from the city center and the wait times due to the high volume of customers.

text.text:
Bakery and restaurant in one 😍

We went there for breakfast and really enjoyed it! There’s an open kitchen where you can watch them bake bread.

I can highly recommend the scrambled eggs with truffle and the bread is just awesome 👌
They offer bread, baguettes, croissants, cakes, as well as regional jams, pickled vegetables, liqueurs, wines, and cheeses, all packaged to take home.

On the first floor you’ll find the restaurant, along with the cabinets of herbs that are grown there.

Go early in the morning or make a reservation. Card payment is possible.

Breakfast is served, but you have to place your order at the counter, you’ll get a little sign with a number to put on your table.

Tucked away in a nice and quiet neighborhood of Prague, V Zátiší is a true gem! We were greeted warmly right from the start and shown to our table with genuine hospitality.
The amuse-bouche was an absolute delight—it literally burst with flavor in the mouth. The fresh bread that followed was just as impressive. For starters, we enjoyed the prawns and an outstanding sea bream ceviche, both beautifully presented and packed with taste. The sea bass main course was perfectly cooked, and the chocolate mousse dessert provided a rich, satisfying finish.
Full marks for the impeccable service, stunning presentation, and the amazing flavors throughout. The lovely ambiance rounded off an unforgettable evening. Highly recommended for anyone seeking a refined yet relaxed dining experience in Prague

text.text: I saw Cafe Savoy listed in a few searches for “best brunch in Prague” so decided to give it a try. This place certainly delivers on tasty food, good service and an eye-catching interior. However, it doesn’t offer anything particularly special or unique, especially for the high prices.  The brunch menu is also quite limited on dishes, with a few different breakfast platters to choose from and some omelets. It’s mostly a spot for tourists visiting the Prague 1/2 area. While our brunch was good, I walked away with really no desire to ever return. For a brunch that’s more local and unique, I’ll stick to the Karlin and Holešovice neighborhoods.

text.text:
A truly elegant and historic café in Prague with a stunning interior. The high ceilings, classic decor, and traditional atmosphere make you feel like you’ve stepped back in time. It’s a perfect place for breakfast, brunch, or a relaxing coffee break.
The desserts are beautifully presented and very delicious. The pastries taste fresh and high quality, and the coffee is rich and well-prepared. Everything we tried was satisfying and carefully served.
Despite being quite popular and sometimes busy, the staff remained professional and attentive. The overall ambiance is refined yet comfortable, making it a great place to enjoy both the atmosphere and the food.
If you’re looking for a classic European café experience in Prague, this place is definitely worth visiting. Highly recommended.

Definitely a nice venue to visit when in Praha.

text.text: We had Valentines dinner here - we had miso soup (more like a broth) with a rich umami flavor and rice noodles (highly recommend). The bbq tofu bao bun was pretty good with a side of wedges and horseradish mayo. My friend had the ‘traditional’ Czech bread dumpling with meat substitute, purée and cranberry gel- it was well flavored - it’s a good dish if you have grown up with Czech food and are looking for a vegetarian version. The apricot lemonade was quite sweet and more like juice than lemonade. (Not sour/tangy enough) Overall, lovely atmosphere (love the mushroom lamps) and the young waiter serving us was friendly with a great smile!

text.text: Visited here as a table of 2 on Wednesday night in January. We had booked but got there half an hour early and they kindly let us have our table right away. We ordered the hummus with bread and goats cheese mousse with bread to start. We then had the jackfruit burritos and Czech Traditional Svíčková (root vegetable sauce with Carlsbad dumplings, plant-based medallion, lemon, and cranberry gel) with a side of potato wedges and a side salad. All were delicious and arrived so quickly! One tiny improvement would be for the bread to be warm with the hummus, and the hummus to be room temperature. I would highly recommend as all food was to such a high standard and my favourite dish was the Czech Traditional Svíčková which I'd suggest to anyway. It was quite busy so I'd also recommend booking in possible. Make sure you give this fav place a try! Thank you team Lehká hlava 🙂.

text.text:
It was a truly unique experience. The restaurant and its ambience are one of a kind and definitely a must-visit when in Prague.

The only downside was related to timing. We had a reservation for 8:45 pm but arrived at the restaurant around 9:05 pm. The staff informed us that 9:00 pm is the last order. They were kind enough to still accommodate us and offered a very limited, shortened menu with only a few options, from which we placed our order.

The food was absolutely delicious, and we have no complaints about the service at all. My only recommendation would be to clearly mention that 9:00 pm is the last order time and to avoid offering reservation slots between 9:00 and 9:30 pm on the booking screen, as this can be misleading.

That said, I would definitely visit again on my next trip to Prague—this time with enough time to explore the full menu.

text.text:
I had heard about Kantýna before my trip to Prague and really wanted to try it. The bank atmosphere is unique, and the gentleman at the entrance was very kind, he gives you the ticket at the start and handles the payment at the end.
I tried the potato soup, which was different from what I’m used to; it was fine, and the meat in it was a nice addition.
However, the real highlight was the beef tartare with garlic and toast, it was definitely the best choice. The portions were generous and the food quality was great.
My only complaint is the staff at the food counter; he was quite indifferent and less than friendly when I ordered.
Despite this, it's a must-visit spot in Prague.

We visited on our last day in Prague to get one final fix of authentic Czech food. Kantýna is something special, it’s a bustling, local spot with a unique atmosphere that feels like a modern take on a traditional butcher hall. The cooked meats were outstanding, and we absolutely loved the Garlic Potato Pancake.

We ordered:
- Garlic Potato Pancake (Crispy and full of flavor!)
- Cooked Meats (Excellent quality and very tender)
- Cooked Vegetables
- Royal Crown Cola
- Pilsner Urquell Pale Lager

text.text:
I was genuinely surprised by how delicious the steak was. I originally didn’t have much of an appetite and usually eat very little, but after tasting the steak, it actually made me want to order another dish. That rarely happens to me.

The red wine was excellent, and the introduction was very professional.

The risotto was of a very high standard — perfectly balanced, well-paired, and not overwhelming at all. I truly loved it.

The staff member who introduced the wine list was extremely professional, and the server who helped us order was very enthusiastic and patient in explaining the menu, which helped us quickly understand the dishes.

This is the restaurant where I have felt the happiest and most comfortable dining experience during my entire Eastern Europe trip. It was also a very touching moment for me — discovering such wonderful food made this journey even more meaningful.

Myself and my wife had the calamari and steak tartare which both were amazing, the long potato chips served with the tartare were excellent. My wife had salsify risotto with belly pork which she loved. I had the venison which was a beautifully cooked piece with a really rich sauce, my only criticism would be I would’ve liked to pour the sauce on the dish myself rather than have it poured by the waitress as it ended up all over the food.
Service up to that point was attentive however after that we were somewhat forgotten and had to call several different waiters, because it was unclear who was actually taking care of our table. We had to ask for desert menus and then unfortunately when we decided on the cheese, it was not available. Overall lovely restaurant and  food but service could’ve been improved.

Bellevue Staré Město.

The richly decorated Dutch Neo-Renaissance house on Smetana Embankment in Prague owes its name to the French because of its location, which offers exceptional views of Hradčany and the Vltava River.
The combination of the words "belle vue" can be translated as "beautiful view."

A house in this style is not very typical of Czech buildings. It was built in the mid-19th century according to a design by the architect Konstantin Mráček.

The dominant feature of the house is the massive prismatic tower with Neo-Renaissance elements and a tall bay window at the corner of the tower.

The top floor formerly housed painters' studios, one of which was occupied for a time by the Austrian painter Oskar Koloschka.

The ground floor housed the luxurious Bellevue restaurant, which has been closed since 2021.

From May to November 1945, this house housed the commanders of the Soviet army, the liberators of the Czechoslovak people.

originalText.text:
Bellevue Staré Město.

Het rijkelijk vesierde huis in Nederlandse neorenaissancesstijl aan de Smetana-dijk in Praag dankt zijn naam aan de Fransen vanwege de ligging , die een uitzonderlijk uitzicht biedt op Hradčany en de  rivier de Moldau.
De combinatie van de woorden  '' belle vue ''  kan vertaald worden als
'' prachtig uitzicht ''.

Een huis in deze stijl is niet erg typerend voor Tsjechische gebouwen . Het werd halverwege de 19de-eeuw gebouwd volgens het ontwerp van de architect Konstantin Mráček.

Het dominante kenmerk van het huis is de massieve prismatische toren met neorenaissance -elementen en een hoge erker op de hoek van de toren.

Op de bovenste verdieping waren vroeger schildersateliers , waarvan er één een tijdlang werd bewoond door de Oostenrijkse schilder Oskar
Koloschka.

Op de begane grond bevond zich het luxe restaurant Bellevue , maar dat is sinds 2021 gesloten.

In dit huis woonden van mei tot november 1945, de commandanten van het Sovjetleger , de bevrijders van het Tsjechoslowaakse volk.

text.text: The Bellevue House is a rare example of the Dutch Renaissance in Prague. Built between 1893 and 1897, the architect was Konstantin Mráček.

originalText.text: Дом Бельвю (Bellevue) - редчайший в Праге образец нидерландского Ренессанса. Построен в 1893-1897 гг. Арх. Константин Мрачек.

text.text:
Had to book well in advance to make sure we got a table here! We never received an email to confirm our reservation so had to rely on memory to make sure we turned up at the right time! So make sure you make a note of what time you're supposed to be there when you book.

Nevertheless the food was very tasty. We ordered a Dish burger and a Chilli burger. Both were good and well presented. One portion of fries was enough between two people.

Drinks service was a little slow, the waiter convinced me into having another beer (which I probably didn't need) towards the end of the meal and it then took ten minutes to arrive (by which point I was beginning to know I didn't need it)

Sauces cost about £1 extra which is strange when you usually expect to get ketchup/mayo/mustard to your heart's content in a burger joint.

They serve a nice local beer (see photo) which is also good value.

However all in all it's about 760czk/£25 for dinner including tip so you can't really go wrong here.

Very great and high quality restaurant!
You need to make a reservation in time to sit inside.
The waiter who served us (black hair and glases near the kitchen) was super friendly and passionate!
The steak prices are on the higher side compared to other countries.
The starters to share were very delicious.

The steak was a bit too far but still juicy.
The waiter suggested us to try the porridge…. and this was a big surprise for us because of how tasty and not heavy the dish was!
Thank you for the great night.

text.text:
This is one of the best restaurants I’ve been to in Prague. The location itself is incredible- right next to the Presidential Palace. It’s also situated on a hill overlooking the city itself.

The restaurant itself is adorable- almost cafeteria style seating with communal tables. However, I never felt too crowded. They have exceptional restrooms as well.

Our server was very kind, knowledgeable, and if you’re nervous she also spoke perfect English. I highly recommend her.

My meal was the meat in dill sauce. The sauce itself was perfect- very dilly. Eggs were cooked well along with the potatoes. The meat was a little dry but the dill sauce helped make the dish complete. My one minor complaint was that it took a long time to be checked out but that may be because I’m an impatient American.

This place really lives up to its reputation in many ways. The panoramic city views are stunning, and the traditional food is flavorful and satisfying.
However, the experience wasn’t perfect. We waited more than an hour for our meals, and the service was quite slow, even getting a menu took some effort.
Be sure to book a reservation in advance; without one, you’ll probably only be able to enjoy a drink on the terrace. Even so, the atmosphere and scenery make it a spot worth visiting at least once.

Alcron Restaurant in Prague is truly exceptional, blending sophisticated atmosphere with world-class hospitality. While known for elegance, their vegetarian dishes stole the show—the crispy potato and fried cauliflower were absolute highlights, alongside a phenomenal beef tartare.
​Our experience was elevated by the incredible service; our waitress, Lisa, was extremely helpful with her spot-on recommendations. We loved it so much that we returned for a second visit!

However, note that the steak price depends on the portion size you are served, so the price listed on the menu can be misleading.
We had a 9:30 pm reservation, yet at 10:15 pm we were told that they were closing since we were the only guests left, which made us feel rushed. The atmosphere was quite gloomy and uninviting. Overall, not a place I would recommend.

text.text:
Disappointing Christmas Eve Dinner

We dined here on December 24th and, unfortunately, our experience was far from what we expected for such a special evening.

Service Issues:
Many guests, including ourselves, were seated in a space close to the hotel lobby. It looked like we were seated in a corridor. The service was extremely slow. It took 1 hour and 15 minutes just to receive an amuse-bouche. Throughout the evening, servers seemed overwhelmed and disorganized. They repeatedly poured still water into glasses where we had sparkling water, and vice versa. (You can clearly see the separation on the photo attached) One server, though friendly, kept visiting our table without saying anything and ended the service looking disheveled. Another server even started limping by the end of the night.

Food Quality:
The amuse-bouche was more of a “mouth rinse” just a mushroom juice. It was good but weirdly served in a cup of coffee which created some confusion at the beginning of the dinner. The fish soup was served cold, as was the next course. The fried fish was reminiscent of a supermarket frozen meal, and the dish was lukewarm at best. We had to ask for bread, and the butter never arrived.

Organization:
The restaurant blamed the slow service on a “rush” in the kitchen, even though the dining room emptied over time. For a fixed menu on Christmas Eve, with reservations only, this can’t be an excuse. The coat check lost a guest’s glove, and at the end of the night, staff asked guests to come retrieve their own coats because they couldn’t find them.

Positive Note:
Despite all these issues, the staff remained friendly throughout the evening. We were offered a 5% discount and complimentary coffee at the end of the meal, which was a small gesture of goodwill.
And we had a good time because we preferred to laugh rather then crying about it!

Conclusion:
While the staff’s friendliness and the small discount were appreciated, the overall experience was disappointing. The food was cold, the service chaotic, and the organization lacking, especially for a Christmas Eve dinner.

text.text:
SERVICE – When I was about to pay, I added a tip and clearly said “3.” After completing the payment, I noticed that “8” was entered on the bill, meaning I was charged an extra 500 CZK. There was no background noise or any other reason that could explain this misunderstanding. Also, if you are not sure you ask again and you show the pos to the client, always! When I stood up to address the issue, the staff member was extremely quick to refund the money, which gave me the impression that he was aware of what he had done.
Additionally, the sommelier was quite pushy when recommending wines and suggested options that were not listed on the menu, without clearly stating their prices by himself. In a former Michelin-starred restaurant located in a five-star hotel, this type of service should not be happening guys. I think you can do much better.
FOOD was generally good, we got tartare - sheep cheese wrapped in chocolate - salmon trout - ravioli - chocolate pavè and we like them all.

text.text: We had the pleasure of celebrating our 45th wedding anniversary here. We had eaten at Noma only two weeks prior so know what an exceptional restaurant can do. I had done a fair bit of research to find the right restaurant in Prague as my wife is a vegetarian and I wanted the meal to be special, and special it was. Simon attended to us and every single dish he served us was as good as what we ate at Noma. The service is impeccable, both in timing of food, and friendliness of the staff, I watched and they provided this level of service for every diner near us. It is not Michelin starred, but it should be, get in quick before it is and the meal prices go up to match the standard of food they serve. My only regret is that we live on the other side of the world and we can’t be regular patrons

editorialSummary.text: Simple Czech restaurant & bar, founded in 1895, with brick walls & a streetside summer terrace.

text.text:
We visited this restaurant in Prague to try traditional Czech cuisine. The main dishes and the salad we ordered were genuinely delicious - credit where it’s due, the food itself was well-prepared and very tasty.

However, there were several disappointing points. The soft drinks we ordered had no carbonation at all - they were completely flat, which was quite surprising.

The restaurant is also quite expensive, so naturally we expected a higher level of attention to detail and transparency.

More importantly, the dessert we ordered contained alcohol, but this was neither clearly stated on the menu nor mentioned by the staff. Restaurants should clearly indicate on the menu when a dish contains alcohol. Proper labeling is important so that guests can make informed choices. Transparency in this matter would have been appreciated.

Another issue was that a 10% service charge was added to the bill without informing us beforehand. We were not told about it, nor were we asked. While we did not argue about it at the time, this kind of charge should be clearly communicated to customers in advance.

Overall, while the main food was good, the lack of transparency, communication, and the high prices left us disappointed.

text.text:
The restaurant has an extremely cozy and inviting atmosphere, with very attentive and polite staff. Everything was very clean and well organized. We had homemade lemonades which were delicious and not overly sweet, which we really appreciated.
For the main course, we had a soup and two different dishes. One of them was a pork dish cooked in beer with traditional bread dumplings. All the meals were very well seasoned and the portions were generous. Everything tasted fresh and flavorful.

Just a small tip for improvement: the main dish had quite a lot of bread because of the traditional dumplings, so perhaps slightly less bread and a bit more meat would make the balance even better. Overall, everything was perfect and delicious.

text.text:
My wife and I were looking for authentic Czech cuisine in Prague, and U KROKA absolutely delivered! From the moment we arrived, the staff was incredibly friendly and accommodating — even though I had mistakenly booked our reservation for the wrong date and the restaurant was fully booked, they still managed to find us a table. That level of kindness and hospitality really set the tone for a wonderful evening.

We tried several traditional dishes and everything was absolutely delicious. The Prague ham with Olomouc curd cheese foam and apple gel was a perfect starter — delicate and flavorful. The Svíčková was rich and comforting, the beef goulash hearty and satisfying, and the dessert, cake žemlovka, was a delightful surprise with its mix of apples, cream cheese, white chocolate, and apple sorbet.

I had a crisp Pilsner beer that paired beautifully with the meal, while my wife enjoyed a refreshing mango lemonade. The menu is varied, so there’s something for everyone, and most of the staff speaks English, which makes it easy for non-Czech speakers to feel welcome and understood.

Highly recommend U KROKA for anyone wanting a genuine taste of Czech cuisine with top-notch service. We’ll definitely be back!

BRICKS Hergatova Cihelna is a unique gem that seamlessly blends rustic charm with modern design. Located in a beautifully restored brick factory, the atmosphere is both warm and inviting, making it an ideal spot for gatherings or a quiet afternoon.

The menu offers a delightful range of dishes that highlight fresh, locally sourced ingredients. Each meal is thoughtfully prepared, showcasing a creative twist on traditional recipes. The standout items often include artisanal pizzas and seasonal specialties that reflect the region’s culinary heritage.

Service at BRICKS is attentive yet relaxed, with staff eager to provide recommendations and ensure a pleasant dining experience. The outdoor seating area, surrounded by greenery, adds to the overall ambiance, making it a lovely spot to enjoy a meal during warmer months.

Overall, BRICKS Hergatova Cihelna is a must-visit for anyone looking to enjoy great food in a charming setting, combining the best of both worlds—historical significance and contemporary flair.

text.text:
Bricks Prague Restaurant completely exceeded our expectations — a true gem in the heart of the city. From the moment we walked in, the ambiance was warm and inviting, with an elegant yet relaxed vibe that made us feel instantly at home.

But what truly made our evening unforgettable was the impeccable service we received from David, our waiter. He was absolutely outstanding — professional, friendly, and incredibly knowledgeable about the menu. His recommendations were spot-on, and he went above and beyond to ensure everything was perfect throughout the night. You could tell he genuinely cared about the experience of each guest, which is rare and so appreciated.

The food itself was phenomenal — beautifully presented, rich in flavor, and made with top-quality ingredients. Every course felt thoughtfully prepared.

Bricks isn’t just a restaurant; it’s an experience — and David made it exceptional. We’ll definitely be returning on our next trip to Prague, and we hope to be served by David again!

The cocktails themselves were exceptional, beautifully balanced, creative, and clearly made with great attention to detail. You can really feel the passion behind every drink, which makes the experience even more special.

Overall, Hemingway Bar combines excellent service, high-quality cocktails, and a unique charm that makes it unforgettable. A perfect place for a classy evening drink and an experience that truly lives up to its reputation.

text.text:
If you love champagne, come here straight from the airport. Prague can wait!

Their choice of grower champagnes is outstanding: deep, diverse, and genuinely well curated. Just tell them what you like - style, mood, preferences, and they’ll find the right bottle for you.

Miloš, the founder, is a true champagne lover and, just as importantly, a great person. He doesn’t drink bad champagne and won’t let you do it either. Some bottles on the list would feel perfectly at home in top-tier restaurants, but why to go there if you can enjoy them here? Add proper Zalto glassware, and you immediately know you’re in good hands.

The bar is well known, so at peak hours you may need to wait. Hence, reserving in advance is a good idea to secure a spot. And don’t limit yourself to just one bottle… Why would you, if you can enjoy a few?

text.text:
Cloud 9 in Prague offers a truly impressive first impression. The entrance to the restaurant is stunning and feels both stylish and exciting. We were warmly welcomed by a staff member at the entrance who personally guided us to our table, which immediately set a great tone for the evening.

At the table, we were given a small box with mini menus inside. A fun and creative touch that made the experience feel a bit special. The staff was quick to check on us and even offered complimentary popcorn, which was a nice surprise.

Our first round of drinks arrived quickly and tasted excellent. The second round took a bit longer, but the staff communicated clearly about the delay and handled the situation very professionally, which we really appreciated.

On top of all that, the view is absolutely beautiful and adds a lot to the overall experience. A great place for drinks if you are looking for atmosphere, service, and a memorable setting.

text.text:
If Prague had a place where sunsets, cocktails, and “wow” moments signed a long-term lease together, it would be Cloud 9 Sky Bar & Lounge.

Perched on the 9th floor of the Hilton Prague, this bar delivers panoramic views of the Vltava River and the city skyline that make you pause mid-conversation and say, “Sorry… look at that.” Floor-to-ceiling windows, outdoor terraces, and golden-hour lighting do most of the flirting for you — especially around sunset and after dark.

The cocktails are creative, beautifully crafted, and dangerously easy to drink. Premium spirits, clever combinations, and presentation that deserves its own photoshoot. Food-wise, expect tasty bites, sharing plates, and burgers that are surprisingly generous for a sky bar — perfect for soaking up one more drink.

The staff are friendly, professional, and genuinely attentive, which makes the whole experience feel smooth and relaxed. Bonus points for thoughtful details like lending parkas when it gets chilly outside — because freezing for a view is only romantic for about five minutes.

Yes, it’s on the pricier side, but this is one of those places where you’re paying for the full experience: views, vibe, service, and atmosphere. Live DJs on Fridays and Saturdays add just the right amount of energy without turning it into a nightclub in the clouds.

Final verdict: A fantastic choice for a special evening in Prague. Come for the views, stay for the cocktails, and leave slightly poorer but significantly happier 🍸🌇✨

text.text:
U Fleků is one of the oldest breweries in Prague, with a documented history going back to 1499.  ￼ The beer here is certainly good, they brew a dark and a light lager on the premises, and either one is worth trying. But the visit left me with mixed feelings. The place is huge and steeped in history, which is cool for a beer-lover or someone just soaking up the atmosphere. That said, the service is really disappointing. The staff act friendly, going around offering beer and snacks at the table, but every “free” pass-by ends up on your bill without you being clearly told upfront. The food is solidly Czech: the potato soup was just okay, the duck and sausages were fine but nothing special, especially for the price. So if you go, go for the beer and the setting, but don’t expect top-tier food or service.
If this review was helpful, leave a 💛

text.text:
The atmosphere was indeed authentic. The chicken was well seasoned and delicious, and the cabbage had a really nice flavor – unfortunately, that’s about all I can say that’s positive about this place. I felt half scammed because I was talked into trying a “traditional” shot, and only after I drank it was I told I had to pay for it. It was quite an expensive shot, and while it didn’t taste bad, the way they “sold” it to me completely ruined my vibe. Overall, the food was pretty meh; the only things I genuinely enjoyed were the half chicken and the cabbage.
When we went to pay by card, I was suddenly told: “I will add a 10% tip, if that’s ok.” NO, that is not ok. I refused to pay the tip because I didn’t have a great experience and, in such cases, I don’t think a tip is deserved. I hate being made to feel obligated to tip when the service doesn’t really earn it.

My recommendation: if you like beer, stop by for a drink, enjoy the medieval atmosphere, and have your meal somewhere else.

text.text:
Saturday night in Prague celebrating my brother's birthday, and we were curious about this club after hearing rave reviews. The experience was truly amazing! Finding it was a bit of an adventure – it's not exactly signposted, and you need to navigate a caged gate and a bouncer to get in. The unique system uses a prepaid card, which you use to buy drinks.

Inside, the club has two distinct vibes: one side featured a DJ spinning house tracks, while the other side showcases a live band. The band was incredible, although things got a bit wild when they encouraged the guys in the crowd to shed their shirts and swing them overhead – maybe a Czech tradition?

Despite the quirks, the club's atmosphere was electric, and I'd highly recommend it to anyone looking for a memorable night out in Prague!

text.text:
If you are looking for an outstanding wine bar in Prague, Vinograf is an absolute must-visit. From the moment you step inside, you can feel the passion for wine. The atmosphere is stylish yet cozy, making it the perfect place for a relaxed evening with friends or a romantic night out.

The wine list is truly impressive, featuring an excellent selection of white and red wines from Germany, France, Italy, and Spain. Whether you prefer a crisp German Riesling, an elegant French Burgundy, a bold Spanish Rioja, or a smooth Italian Sangiovese, there is something for every palate. Both wine enthusiasts and casual drinkers will appreciate the diversity and quality offered here.

The knowledgeable and friendly staff are always happy to recommend the perfect glass to match your taste. To complement the wines, the bar also serves delicious small dishes and snacks that enhance the overall tasting experience.

Gänsehosen

text.text:
This attraction is best visited during the day and early evenings at sunset. It is also nice to take a boat tour and view the bridge and the old town from the river.

At night, it is very dimly lit, almost without light so it’s hard to appreciate the bridge. The surrounding views of the old town and castle flooded by golden spotlights are an enchanting sight from the bridge though.

Early mornings before 0700 is also a magical hour to watch the daybreak and skies waking to a rosy hue. At this hour, the bridge is quiet with few people. This is the best time to have the bridge almost to yourself.

We were lucky enough to visit several times and even got to see the gentleman who manually lights the lamps during the Christmas season, which was a really special touch. Even with all the people, walking across the bridge is unforgettable and gives you a real sense of Prague’s history and beauty.

editorialSummary.text: Medieval timepiece on the facade of city hall displaying the twelve apostles as the clock strikes.

text.text:
The Prague Astronomical Clock is absolutely breathtaking and one of those places that reminds you why old Europe is special. Standing in Old Town Square, you can feel the history — this clock has been marking time for centuries, and it still works beautifully today.

The hourly show is simple but powerful, not flashy, and that’s exactly the charm. It’s traditional, mechanical, and deeply rooted in history — something modern attractions simply can’t replicate. The details on the clock face, the astronomical elements, and the surrounding architecture are incredible, especially if you take a moment to really look.

Visit it during the day to appreciate the craftsmanship, and again at night when the atmosphere is magical and less crowded. It’s free to see, timeless in spirit, and truly one of Prague’s greatest treasures. A must-see, no excuses.

text.text:
St. Vitus Cathedral is one of the most remarkable and sought-after cathedrals in Europe. With its history spanning several centuries and construction carried out across different eras, the cathedral beautifully blends multiple architectural styles, resulting in a grandeur that is truly hard to put into words.
Predominantly Gothic in style, the cathedral is defined by its soaring spires, pointed arches, flying buttresses, and stunning stained-glass windows that flood the interiors with color and light. Later additions introduce subtle Renaissance and Baroque elements, reflecting the long timeline over which it was completed. Every detail, from the intricate stonework to the ornate chapels and tombs, speaks volumes about its historical and artistic significance.
To fully enjoy the experience, it is best to arrive early in the day to avoid heavy crowds and long waiting times. Picking up an audio guide is highly recommended, as it allows you to immerse yourself deeply in the cathedral’s history, architecture, and symbolism at your own pace, making the visit far more enriching and memorable.

text.text:
St. Vitus Cathedral (Katedrála sv. Víta) is the spiritual heart of Prague and an unforgettable highlight of any visit to the city. Rising majestically within Prague Castle, the cathedral impresses immediately with its soaring Gothic architecture and intricate stonework. Inside, the vast nave feels both grand and serene, drawing visitors upward toward its ribbed vaults and towering columns. The stained-glass windows are breathtaking, especially the Art Nouveau window by Alfons Mucha, which floods the interior with rich color and light. The cathedral also holds deep historical significance, serving as the coronation site of Czech kings and the resting place of saints, monarchs, and archbishops. Climbing the Great South Tower rewards visitors with panoramic views over Prague’s red rooftops and the Vltava River. St. Vitus Cathedral is not only an architectural masterpiece, but also a powerful symbol of Czech history, faith, and national identity.

Inside St. Vitus Cathedral (Katedrála sv. Víta) you’ll find a rich combination of art, history, and sacred spaces:
• The High Altar – Located at the eastern end, it dominates the cathedral and reflects its religious importance.
• St. Wenceslas Chapel – One of the most beautiful areas, decorated with semi‑precious stones and medieval frescoes depicting the life of St. Wenceslas, the patron saint of Bohemia.
• Stained‑glass windows – Many are masterpieces, including the famous Art Nouveau window designed by Alfons Mucha, filling the interior with vivid color.
• Royal Tombs and Crypt – Burial place of Czech kings, queens, saints, and Holy Roman Emperors, including Charles IV.
• The Crown Chamber – Holds the Czech Crown Jewels (not always open to the public).
• The Great Organ – A monumental instrument used during major ceremonies.
• Gothic vaults and columns – Towering stone architecture that creates a sense of awe and vertical grandeur.

displayName.text: Church of Our Lady before Týn

editorialSummary.text: 14th-century landmark with 80m towers, ornately carved exteriors & a baroque altarpiece.

text.text:
The Church of Our Lady before Týn stands as one of Prague’s most remarkable Gothic monuments, towering over Old Town Square with an almost storybook presence. Its distinctive twin spires dominate the skyline, creating one of the city’s most recognizable and dramatic views.

Dating back to the 14th century, the church captivates visitors with its imposing exterior and intricately adorned interior.

originalText.text:
The Church of Our Lady before Týn stands as one of Prague’s most remarkable Gothic monuments, towering over Old Town Square with an almost storybook presence. Its distinctive twin spires dominate the skyline, creating one of the city’s most recognizable and dramatic views.

Dating back to the 14th century, the church captivates visitors with its imposing exterior and intricately adorned interior.

text.text:
It was once a political weapon, not just a church. In the 15th century, this church became the main symbolic stronghold of the Hussite movement, a Czech religious reform movement that openly challenged the Catholic Church a full century before Martin Luther. At its peak the chalice symbol (Hussite emblem) was placed prominently on the church façade, Catholic symbols were removed and sermons here openly promoted religious rebellion. This made the church radically controversial in its time.

That golden Virgin statue has changed sides. Look closely at the façade today you see a golden statue of the Virgin Mary. Originally, this spot held a giant golden chalice (Hussite symbol). When Catholic control was restored after the Battle of White Mountain (1620) the chalice was removed, melted down and reused to create the Marian statue you see now. Same gold. Opposite ideology.

The towers are intentionally unequal. Those famous twin spires? They are not the same size. The southern tower (“Adam”) is slightly taller than the northern (“Eve”). Symbolism: Adam leads Eve, medieval theology carved into skyline geometry. Once you know this, you can’t unsee it.

Burials inside the church. including plague victims. The floor hides 60+ graves, including victims of medieval plagues, and people were literally buried under where worshippers stood. Why? Burial inside equals spiritual prestige. Plague years overwhelmed cemeteries. Those worn stone slabs aren’t decorative, they’re foot traffic over graves.

text.text:
The Church of Our Lady before Týn in Prague is nothing short of breathtaking. ⛪

Rising dramatically above Old Town Square, its twin Gothic spires dominate the skyline like something from a fairytale. They don’t just define Prague’s silhouette—they embody it.

Architectural Majesty
Built primarily in the 14th century, the church is a masterpiece of Gothic design. The soaring towers, intricate stonework, and commanding presence create a sense of awe even before you step inside. It feels both powerful and delicate, dramatic yet harmonious.

Interior Beauty
Inside, the church reveals a rich and layered history. The vaulted ceilings, detailed altars, and stunning Baroque elements blend centuries of artistry. The tomb of astronomer Tycho Brahe rests here, adding intellectual and historical depth to its spiritual grandeur.

Atmosphere & Setting
What makes it even more magical is its setting. Emerging from behind a row of Old Town houses, the church feels almost secretive—as if you’ve discovered something sacred tucked within the bustling square. At night, when illuminated, it becomes truly enchanting.

Cultural Significance
For centuries, it has stood as a symbol of Prague’s religious and cultural evolution. It has witnessed reformations, wars, and revolutions—yet it remains steadfast and magnificent.

The Church of Our Lady before Týn isn’t just a building; it’s a crown jewel of Prague’s Old Town and a powerful reminder of the city’s medieval soul.

editorialSummary.text: Home to 12,000 tombstones but many more burials, including scholar & teacher Judah Loew ben Bezalel.

text.text: We visited the Jewish Cemetery independent of a guide as part of our self-guided tour of the Jewish Quarter in Prague.  While we preferred the freedom of not having a guide, we were a little sad we didn't have one, as we wanted to learn more during our visit to the cemetery.  The Cemetery is beautiful and moving, and a must-see site.  However, there is no brochure or signs or anything to help give history of context as you are moving through.  So either bring a guide or bring a guidebook, if that matters to you.  That being said - a very worthy and beautiful place to visit.  So quiet and serene (except the occasional less-than-respectful tour group).  Note: entrance is included as part of the "Jewish Museum" ticket - you cannot pay to enter just this site.  It is 500 czk for entrance to all the sites.

editorialSummary.text: Grand museum established in 1818 with a range of natural science & history exhibits, plus events.

text.text:
National Museum Prague: A Monumental Treasury of Czech Heritage

The National Museum Prague is not just a building; it is a monumental institution that serves as the ultimate repository of Czech history, natural science, and culture. My visit left me profoundly impressed by its sheer scale and the incredible diversity housed within its walls.

The first striking element is the main building itself—the Historical Building on Wenceslas Square. Its Neo-Renaissance architecture is breathtakingly grand, hinting at the vastness of the collections inside.

The diversity of the exhibitions is truly remarkable. Visitors can seamlessly transition from:
* The glittering Mineralogy and Petrology displays, showcasing thousands of specimens with intricate detail.
* To the fascinating Palaeontology hall, which includes the dramatic skeleton of a whale and ancient fossils.
* To the deeply meaningful History sections, meticulously documenting Czech national development and pivotal historical events with great care and detail.

What sets this museum apart is the commitment to detail in presentation. Every exhibit, whether it is a tiny archaeological artifact or a large geological sample, is contextualized and beautifully lit, inviting close examination. It truly feels like walking through an entire encyclopedia brought to life.

If you are looking for an experience that offers both majestic architecture and an exhaustive, diverse, and detailed look into Central European natural and human history, the National Museum Prague is an absolute must-see.

text.text:
Oh I really hated this place.

Let’s start with the good things:

1. The building is beautiful.

The end.

Now unto the bad:

1. The building IS beautiful so it feels like a waste of space having such a poor collection. The museum is mostly a natural museum, with lots of animals and bugs, also a bunch of rocks. Interesting, but that’s almost all there is in the museum. Whatever artworks or sculptures they had were very little and displayed quite weirdly.

2. Every museum always has a religion section, which I never find interesting but having so little to look at in this place I spent more time there as usual, and noticed the absolute LACK of cleaning/taking care of the art. I’ve never been to a museum where I actually noticed the dirtiness and dust accumulating because, I’m pretty sure, the museums I’ve been actually take care of the pieces. But not this one.

3. The gift shop was like the rest of the museum: poor. Very little stuff and expensive. I picked up a postcard that didn’t look too bad to buy but there was a minimum of 50 CZK to pay with card (lol), so I just left it there. At that point I had been in the city for a while and I hadn’t had any issues with paying with card, no minimum.

4. The ticket price is about 15€, which considering how LITTLE they have to show and the absolute LACK of tending to the art is ridiculous they would charge that much. Luckily I found 10€ outside the building so the ticket was almost free. Girl math.

Overall a bad experience. I was already having a bad day when I visited and hoped this museum would cheer me up. It did the opposite.

There are many other museums/local points in the city which are beautiful, cheaper and actually worth seeing. Avoid this place.

text.text:
National Museum, Prague
Where history breathes, and the soul stands still
Stepping into the National Museum in Prague felt like walking into the heart of time itself.
From the moment we stood beneath the magnificent glass dome, light pouring down like a silent blessing, we knew this was not just a museum—it was a cathedral of memory, art, and humanity.
The grand staircase, framed by golden lamps and marble arches, leads you not simply upward, but inward—into centuries of stories, struggles, and triumphs. Every wall, every fresco, every sculpture seems to whisper the voices of those who lived before us.
The painted halls feel like frozen symphonies.
Figures from the past gather in eternal conversation, their expressions alive, their emotions timeless. Beneath the dome, history does not sleep—it breathes.
From the upper gallery, looking out over Prague’s endless red rooftops, the city unfolds like a living manuscript. Past and present meet here, reminding us that we are only a single page in a much larger story.
Standing before this masterpiece, we felt small—yet deeply connected.
Connected to the artists who dreamed, the builders who believed, and the generations who walked these same steps before us.
The National Museum is not just a place you visit.
It is a place that stays with you.
A monument not only of stone and glass, but of human spirit.
Prague, through this museum, speaks to the soul.

text.text:
Petřín Tower is one of Prague’s most charming viewpoints and a wonderful spot to take in the city from above. Often compared to a mini Eiffel Tower, it offers amazing panoramic views of Prague’s red rooftops, the Vltava River, and the surrounding hills.
Visiting around sunset is highly recommended, as the soft golden light gradually transforms into a beautiful evening skyline, making the climb especially rewarding. You can reach the top either by stairs or elevator, and the views from the observation deck are well worth the effort.
Another big advantage is its proximity to Strahov Monastery, which makes it easy to cover both attractions in a single visit. Pairing Petřín Tower with a walk through the gardens and a stop at Strahov creates a relaxed and scenic half-day experience, perfect for those looking to explore Prague beyond the usual tourist trail.

text.text:
Petřín Lookout Tower offers a truly beautiful and phenomenal view of the city. When I visited, the crowd was moderate and well manageable. There are two ticket options available: the more expensive one includes going up by elevator, while the cheaper option is to climb the stairs.

The stairs are fine overall, but I would not recommend using them during the winter, as they can be slippery. The view is absolutely worth it. There is an enclosed viewing area around the middle of the tower that offers a panoramic view, and another enclosed area at the very top. It can be quite cold at the top, and on windy days you can feel the tower gently swaying, which is a bit surprising but also part of the experience.

text.text: The Petrin Tower is an iconic steel lookout tower on Petrin Hill in Prague. It was built in 1891 for the Jubilee Exhibition, inspired by the Eiffel Tower of Paris. Standing about 63.5 meters tall, it offers panoramic views of Prague, accessible via 299 steps or an elevator. Earlier there used to be a funicular train to reach the tower and hill but due to safety reasons it is currently suspended. So one has to do a good hike to reach the tower. Entry ticket costs 250 CZK and extra 150 CZK if you want to access elevator. The views from the top are quite breathtaking. Totally recommended to visit this in Prague.

text.text: The price for the library was 180 Czech crowns, but you are not allowed to actually enter the library room, you can only stand in the doorway. They say it’s because they want to “protect the books”, and I understand that, but for that price, they could at least allow to step inside the room to get a better view, while still maintaining a 2-3 meter distance from the books for their protection. When i saw that i could only have a look from the doorway, i felt like i got a bit ripped off. The libraries seem very beautiful though.

Visiting Strahov Monastery was an absolute delight and one of the most beautiful experiences in Prague. The interior architecture and the paintings are truly out of this world — breathtakingly detailed, elegant, and incredibly well preserved. Every room feels like stepping into a masterpiece.

What made the visit even more memorable was the kindness and professionalism of the staff. We accidentally purchased a larger visiting package than we needed, and the staff were unbelievably nice and understanding. They changed our tickets without any hassle and even refunded the difference to us in cash. That level of honesty and customer care is rare and deeply appreciated.

Thank you very much for such a wonderful experience!

Overall a very nice and cosy place to stay in while you're on holiday, though the location is not as central like some other places, it is in the new town but still very close to the old town square.

We stayed for a total of 6 days, sunday until friday.

editorialSummary.text: Colorful wall featuring Communist-era political slogans, peace messages & a portrait of John Lennon.

text.text:
An interesting place to visit in Prague on the Mala Strana side of Charles Bridge. I discovered this place by chance on Google Maps and was intrigued to go and have a look as I didn't look it up beforehand so I didn't know what to expect.

Upon first glance, I was a bit underwhelmed, it just looked like a wall full of graffiti and defaced. However upon closer inspection there are parts of the wall adorned in art or words/quotes which stand out and aren't just tags.

When I went in Sept 2025, there was a large group which I suspect was a school trip so very busy.

It was interesting to visit simply to see what the memorial looked like and what was on the wall. If you spend a little time you can decipher some of the messages on the wall. I saw things like "Love" and biblical verses which stood out to me as a spiritual person.

Only go out of your way to visit this place if you have time and have a general curiosity to see it otherwise you may feel you have wasted your time and come away very underwhelmed.

There was a plaque by the gateway which said not to deface the wall but unfortunately because the rule is not enforced, anyone can leave their mark hence much of the scrawls on the wall mostly being graffiti.

You don't need to purchase a ticket to visit the wall. This is an outdoor place!

text.text:
​A Living Symbol of Hope and Freedom
​Tucked away in a quiet square near the French Embassy, the Lennon Wall is one of the most vibrant spots in Prague. While many come just for the colorful photos, the real magic is in the history. In the 1980s, this wall was a site of 'silent' rebellion against the communist regime—a place where students risked everything to scrawl lyrics about peace and freedom.
​What I loved most is that it’s never the same twice. It’s a living, breathing canvas of global messages, Beatles lyrics, and street art. Even if you aren't a die-hard John Lennon fan, you can feel the energy of decades of people wanting to leave a positive mark on the world. It’s a beautiful, gritty, and soulful contrast to the medieval architecture of the rest of the city.
​It’s located just a short walk from Charles Bridge. Look for the "Love Lock" bridge nearby on your way there.
​Best Time: Go early in the morning (before 9:00 AM) if you want a photo without the crowds.
​Don't just look at the big murals; some of the most moving parts are the tiny handwritten notes from travelers all over the world.

editorialSummary.text: Historic pilgrimage destination with a church, a cloister, a clock tower & rare liturgical objects.

text.text:
Very interesting place. The 6 chapels, cloisters and church  provide a mind altering visual spectacle not to be missed. The beautiful frescos, painted ceilings, golden baroque detailing.... 👨‍🍳😗 The 1st floor  (second floor to Americans) contains stunning but bit out of the ordinary objects, from the artwork to the intricate monstrance (reliquaries) were real Oddities which exactly why I loved it. The grounds were immaculate and the architecture of the cloisters and the reliefs of Santa Casa are a interesting detail. Unfortunately, I didn't get the time to properly investigate and appreciate most of the first floor as we were rushed out promptly at closing. I guess that why I have it 4 stars. I came across the Loretto after a late lunch and approached the woman outside, I ask what time it closed and she said 5, it was a little after 4 so I said if come back to I could take me time. She persuaded me to go ahead that there want a lot to see. So against my better judgement I went with it. I should have know it would be enough time. Would it's not a huge collection, I go to learn about so I read and I take time to study each piece so I can soak it all up. I only made it to the entry hall of the first floor but was gently reminded it was close to closing as they started turning off the lights. I certainly understand the perspective of want to get people out so you can go home but if that will be the case maybe put a "last entry" time so patrons can decide if it's enough time. While this want a pilgrimage for me personally I do believe the other family present, it was sometime special as they prayed at many of the alters.
So my parting advice is that if you are a person who likes to stand and study objects or if you are coming as a personal pilgrimage come earlier in the day.

text.text: This beautiful Baroque monastery is located near Prague Castle. It is a place of pilgrimage to the Virgin Mary with the Baroque Church of the Nativity, richly decorated in Rococo style with many frescoes and cherubs, and a copy of the Holy House, surrounded by cloisters and chapels. The tower houses a carillon of 27 bells that performs the Loreto service (every hour from 10:00 to 17:00). The Loreto treasure includes a rare collection of liturgical objects and other votive offerings from the 16th to 18th centuries, the most famous of which is the "Prague Sun" - a tabernacle encrusted with 6,222 diamonds.

text.text: The Powder Tower is one of Prague’s most iconic Gothic landmarks and a historic entrance to the Old Town. Built in the late 15th century, it was once part of the Royal Route and later used to store gunpowder. You can climb inside via a spiral staircase, where you’ll see Gothic interiors, historical displays, and impressive stone details. At the top, there’s a nice panoramic view over the Old Town. Entry is ticketed, but well worth it for the history and views.

The Powder Gate Tower is a beautiful historic landmark and one of Prague’s most iconic sights. Originally serving as an old gateway to the Old Town, it carries centuries of history within its Gothic architecture. The intricate details and towering presence make it a must-see for anyone exploring the city.
By day, the tower looks impressive and majestic, standing as a proud reminder of Prague’s medieval past. At night, it becomes even more enchanting, illuminated against the backdrop of the city lights, creating a magical atmosphere perfect for photos and evening strolls.
If you’re visiting Prague, don’t miss the chance to admire this architectural gem and even climb to the top for stunning views of the Old Town.

text.text:
Disappointing Christmas Eve Dinner

We dined here on December 24th and, unfortunately, our experience was far from what we expected for such a special evening.

Service Issues:
Many guests, including ourselves, were seated in a space close to the hotel lobby. It looked like we were seated in a corridor. The service was extremely slow. It took 1 hour and 15 minutes just to receive an amuse-bouche. Throughout the evening, servers seemed overwhelmed and disorganized. They repeatedly poured still water into glasses where we had sparkling water, and vice versa. (You can clearly see the separation on the photo attached) One server, though friendly, kept visiting our table without saying anything and ended the service looking disheveled. Another server even started limping by the end of the night.

Food Quality:
The amuse-bouche was more of a “mouth rinse” just a mushroom juice. It was good but weirdly served in a cup of coffee which created some confusion at the beginning of the dinner. The fish soup was served cold, as was the next course. The fried fish was reminiscent of a supermarket frozen meal, and the dish was lukewarm at best. We had to ask for bread, and the butter never arrived.

Organization:
The restaurant blamed the slow service on a “rush” in the kitchen, even though the dining room emptied over time. For a fixed menu on Christmas Eve, with reservations only, this can’t be an excuse. The coat check lost a guest’s glove, and at the end of the night, staff asked guests to come retrieve their own coats because they couldn’t find them.

Positive Note:
Despite all these issues, the staff remained friendly throughout the evening. We were offered a 5% discount and complimentary coffee at the end of the meal, which was a small gesture of goodwill.
And we had a good time because we preferred to laugh rather then crying about it!

Conclusion:
While the staff’s friendliness and the small discount were appreciated, the overall experience was disappointing. The food was cold, the service chaotic, and the organization lacking, especially for a Christmas Eve dinner.

SERVICE – When I was about to pay, I added a tip and clearly said “3.” After completing the payment, I noticed that “8” was entered on the bill, meaning I was charged an extra 500 CZK. There was no background noise or any other reason that could explain this misunderstanding. Also, if you are not sure you ask again and you show the pos to the client, always! When I stood up to address the issue, the staff member was extremely quick to refund the money, which gave me the impression that he was aware of what he had done.
Additionally, the sommelier was quite pushy when recommending wines and suggested options that were not listed on the menu, without clearly stating their prices by himself. In a former Michelin-starred restaurant located in a five-star hotel, this type of service should not be happening guys. I think you can do much better.
FOOD was generally good, we got tartare - sheep cheese wrapped in chocolate - salmon trout - ravioli - chocolate pavè and we like them all.

When I stood up to address the issue, the staff member was extremely quick to refund the money, which gave me the impression that he was aware of what he had done.
Additionally, the sommelier was quite pushy when recommending wines and suggested options that were not listed on the menu, without clearly stating their prices by himself. In a former Michelin-starred restaurant located in a five-star hotel, this type of service should not be happening guys. I think you can do much better.
FOOD was generally good, we got tartare - sheep cheese wrapped in chocolate - salmon trout - ravioli - chocolate pavè and we like them all.

text.text: We had the pleasure of celebrating our 45th wedding anniversary here. We had eaten at Noma only two weeks prior so know what an exceptional restaurant can do. I had done a fair bit of research to find the right restaurant in Prague as my wife is a vegetarian and I wanted the meal to be special, and special it was. Simon attended to us and every single dish he served us was as good as what we ate at Noma. The service is impeccable, both in timing of food, and friendliness of the staff, I watched and they provided this level of service for every diner near us. It is not Michelin starred, but it should be, get in quick before it is and the meal prices go up to match the standard of food they serve. My only regret is that we live on the other side of the world and we can’t be regular patrons

text.text:
We had an absolutely wonderful experience at Café Louvre in Prague. The food was truly amazing and exceeded our expectations. Everything we tried was prepared perfectly and full of flavour.

The apple crumble dessert was outstanding — one of the best we’ve ever had. The coffee was excellent, and we especially highly recommend the cappuccino. It was rich, smooth, and perfectly balanced.

We also loved their special Crown Cola, which is made exclusively for them. It had a unique and refreshing taste that made the whole experience even more memorable.

If you’re in Prague, this place is definitely worth visiting. A fantastic atmosphere, delicious food, and high-quality drinks — we would absolutely come back again!

text.text:
The menu seems to be changed seasonally. I asked for recommendations for traditional Czech desserts after seeing the temporary menu, and they said the only options were poppy seed bread and cream sauce.

And although it's not a traditional Czech dessert, I heard that the ricotta cheese peach cake is popular, so I ordered that too.

Both were less expensive than I expected, but I was a little disappointed by the slightly artificial flavor of the dairy products. I also wish they weren't as sweet as the homemade ones.

Poppy Seed tastes like something from a Korean rice cake, is it really a traditional Czech version? The cream sauce that came with it was nice and warm. However, I don't think the taste was not that slightly nutty and rich.

The same thing happened with ricotta cheese cake. It tasted like slightly something artificial and sweet yogurt. It would be better to improve the quality of the ricotta cheesecake even if it reduces the size. The sprinkled hazelnuts were nice.

I suggest selling traditional Czech desserts that celebrities have eaten when they visited in the past.

I was looking forward to trying the usual food from a place that has been around for a long time with history and tradition, so there was no need to prepare anything else.

We visited Café Louvre and it truly felt like stepping back in time. The elegant décor, the refined service, and the way the food was presented on beautifully crafted vintage-style tableware all gave us the feeling of returning to another era.
We ordered the Czech breakfast and the fit breakfast, both of which perfectly combined sweet and savory flavors. The portions were very generous and satisfying. We also had a croissant and cappuccino, and in total we spent around 35 euros.
It was a beautiful experience, and we would definitely recommend it to anyone visiting Prague who wants to enjoy not only good food but also a charming, historic atmosphere.

text.text:
⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️
Café Louvre – Prague

We visited Café Louvre on a Saturday around noon. The line was quite long, but it moved quickly and smoothly, which we really appreciated. The staff is clearly experienced and well organized.

Service was efficient, attentive, and polished. The waiters were smartly dressed, adding to the timeless, elegant atmosphere. We were fortunate to be seated by a window, which made the experience even more enjoyable.

The food was exceptional — beautifully prepared, full of flavor, and deeply satisfying. It was the kind of meal that instantly lifts your mood and makes you slow down and enjoy every bite. You can feel the pride and care that goes into both the kitchen and the service.

Café Louvre may be well known and popular with visitors, but it more than earns its reputation. It offers a wonderful combination of history, ambiance, and high-quality food, all delivered with seamless service.

We would absolutely recommend stopping here when visiting Prague. It’s a classic experience that truly lives up to its name.

text.text:
You can add one more star if you are planning to visit for a coffee only.

The atmosphere and interior are great: classy, bright, great view from the windows, even the bathrooms feel, like you are in an older spy movie. Sipping on a drink, while looking at busy street here feels good and right.

The food however is disappointing: we ordered goulash and svíčková and both were underwhelming. The taste was ok, but nothing special.
The portion size however (it’s small), does not match the plate size.
Food also has a very basic look - considering the price of both, I found it unsatisfactory and frankly sad. A place in such a location and with such a venue can definitely do better, especially if they charge 300+czk per meal.

The drinks are fine - the eggnog coffee was good and I like, that it came with a glass of water.

The service was odd: the main waiter was good, polite and nice - nothing to complain about. The supporting staff however left me puzzled: one lady knocked off my knife and instead of apologising and/or bringing a new one, she simply picked it up and walked away.
And another waiter, who brought us the dessert, did not greet us, seemingly did not acknowledge our existence and stood there with a plate waiting for us to direct him to the person ordering the dessert.

Overall, a disappointing experience. Left me with a feeling of former glory.

text.text:
It's a place right in front of the river, but don't expect much of a view. The atmosphere was great, felt like a hotel restaurant. The food was amazing. It was the best goulash I have ever had. The fried onion pieces and tiny berries in the soup made it so flavorful. It was expensive, but I'd say it was pretty much worth the price.

I'm giving 3 stars because of the service. Everyone were friendly but they all seemed to be busy and took a while to reach me. It seemed like tip was mandatory, and they let me choose between 10, 15, 20%. I didn't really feel like tipping them that much but I selected 10%. But I got charged with slightly more than that, which was almost 12%. I have noticed that right after I paid, but the waitress left so quickly I couldn't ask her about it. I just left since it wasn't a big amount and I did not want to make a fuss about it. But it would be nice to get a clear explanation about the amount I pay if it's included for some reason, or the staff should be more cautious when calculating the prices. I hope she did not charge me more tips on purpose, underestimating a young customer.
