text.text:
Cafe Slavia is a historic cafe in Prague where Nazım Hikmet wrote some of his poems during his years in exile. The atmosphere and the view are absolutely magnificent.
Its hard to describe how special this place feels.
I sat at the table next to Nazım’s portrait, sipping my coffee and simply watching the trams and people passing by outside the window. It was such a peaceful and inspiring experience.
This is truly one of the best cafes I’ve ever visited. The coffee is delicious, and the staff are very attentive and friendly.
I loved it so much that I came back the next day and sat at the same table again. As long as I’m in Prague, this will be my go-to place to enjoy coffee and watch the trams from the window.

The food is impressive and high-quality — I liked it so much I visited twice, once for breakfast and once for lunch. For breakfast, I recommend choosing the buffet; you get a great variety of dishes for a reasonable price. Lunch was also excellent, but it’s best to make a reservation. Their coffee and desserts are lovely as well. With its elegant interiors and warm service, this place is absolutely worth a visit.

text.text:
We stopped by Grand Café Orient to see the famous cubist café. It’s located on the first floor, and when we arrived we were able to find a table right away, even though the place was quite busy.

We ordered coffee, tea, and the well-known cubist dessert. The service was prompt and friendly, which we appreciated. The dessert was tasty, though it didn’t seem as fresh as we had hoped.

One thing to note: when it came time to pay, the staff strongly encouraged paying in cash, and only hesitantly accepted a card.

As for the overall experience, once inside there was nothing particularly special that stood out. It’s interesting to visit once to see what the hype is about, but in the end it’s simply a café/restaurant without much to distinguish it beyond its reputation.

Overall, worth stopping by out of curiosity, but not a place we’d go out of our way to return to.

Elevator available for those with mobility issues. We were able to pay with credit card with no insistence on cash.

text.text:
We stopped by for drinks and dessert, and were immediately struck by the beauty of the space—the expansive windows, the wooden grand piano, and the elegant cubist-inspired décor create a truly memorable atmosphere.

We ordered Turkish coffee, ginger tea, and their Grand Café Orient, a light chocolate cake served with English mint cream. The Turkish coffee was pleasant; although it was unexpectedly served in a tall glass, the presentation didn’t affect the flavor, so no complaints there. The ginger tea was refreshing, and the handy little lemon-squeezing device was a surprisingly fun touch.

As for the dessert, I have mixed impressions. The Grand Café Orient cake is visually stunning, and the English mint cream was a delightful surprise—smooth, subtle, and refined, avoiding the common mistake of making it taste like toothpaste.😊 However, the cake itself leaned a bit dry. It was indeed as light as promised, which kept it from feeling overly rich or heavy, but a touch more moisture would have elevated it further.

The staff were warm and efficient, and we appreciated the dessert display near the counter. It would be even better if the individual items were labeled, as it would help guests identify the treats at a glance.

Overall, a lovely experience in a beautifully designed café.

text.text:
Kavárna Obecní dům is a wonderful place with a beautiful historic atmosphere that truly reflects the charm of Prague. The interior is elegant and full of history, making it a unique and memorable experience from the moment you walk in. It’s a quiet and relaxing café-restaurant, often accompanied by live piano music, which adds to the refined and peaceful ambiance.

The menu offers excellent traditional Czech meals, but there is also a wide variety of options, so everyone can easily find a personal favorite. Everything we tried was well prepared and very enjoyable. The desserts are especially worth mentioning and are a must-have when paired with their great coffee.

We really enjoyed spending time here while meeting friends, as it’s a perfect place for conversation, good food, and a calm, elegant setting. Highly recommended for anyone looking to experience classic Prague style, great cuisine, and a relaxing atmosphere. Five stars without hesitation.

text.text:
Coffee is good and of good quality. We ordered flat white - can recommend.

They don’t really to takeaway coffee - I think it would be great for them to let people know well in advance. Either you come with the reusable cup - or you lend a reusable cup from them (you get your money back when you return it), no single-use cups. They claim it for the ecology, but I doubt the efficiency of this approach and would love to learn if this really works (or how lending reusable cups help). In short - they need to tell people this well in advance somehow - eg sign on the door.

Sitting inside is ok. There is a big communal table and a little space near the windows. For me it wasn’t too cozy though, but you have very few options.

Service is friendly and professional.

text.text:
We’ve been coming to this place a few times on different trips, and it always feels a bit different from other spots. The decor and vibe stand out — there’s a giant table in the middle, seating by the windows, a cozy area at the bottom of the stairs, and some outdoor seating as well. It’s almost always busy with customers, which is always a good sign.

The drinks are consistently great, and the pastries we’ve tried have been very good too. While we usually stop by just for drinks, one pastry in particular — topped with fresh raspberries (see the photo) — was absolutely delightful. The tartness of the raspberry adds such a refreshing touch that the first bite brought an instant smile to my face.

All in all, it’s a lovely spot for drinks and catching up with friends, or a relaxing stop if you can come when it’s a bit quieter.

text.text:
Our first trip to Prague and our first early breakfast in the city - and this turned out to be the best cafe we visited. Very kind staff and a really tasty breakfast. The salmon toast was excellent, the guacamole was done right, and the Eggs Benedict soufflé was a delight - I’d never seen that before, very tasty and unusual.
What impressed me most was the attentiveness. I have lactose intolerance, and people often forget about it. I wanted a matcha, but the milk options were regular or oat, and I really don’t like oat milk. I was ready to take a lactase supplement and go with regular milk, but our server offered pea milk instead. I’d never seen that - it was delicious. When we later ordered a dessert with lactose, she checked again to make sure I’d be okay.
The atmosphere is very pleasant: nice room, comfy tables and chairs, a clean restroom, and an inner summer courtyard. Prices are also friendly - very affordable for Prague. I definitely recommend this place.

I really like the industrial atmosphere. The wear and tear inside looks wholesome and especially good under some sunshine. Reminds me of ruin bars in Budapest, but cosier and coffee focused.

The service is very pleasant and the coffee is good.
Can’t say much about the food - only tried one dish (pancakes) and they were nice: not too sweet, big portion. Also tried couple of different desserts - liked Misha the most - great combo of sweet and sour.

One of the reasons to come back would be to try more breakfast options or to eat lunch, another - to enjoy the atmosphere again!

text.text:
I came here with Tomi after our epic long walk from Prosek all the way through Petřín – we definitely needed good coffee and food after that! The café has such a nice vibe and interesting architecture, and the feeling of young, positive people was exactly what we needed. The food was good, the coffee quite good, and it was a bit cold inside in January, but honestly nothing bad – just added to the authentic industrial vibe.

Kavárna co hledá jméno (literally "Café Looking for a Name") is a brilliant urban revitalization project by Lukáš Žďárský and Jakub Zajíc, two young entrepreneurs who are famous in Prague for transforming abandoned buildings into vibrant social spaces. Both studied at NEWTON University (Lukáš in Management of International Institutions and Public Administration, Jakub in Global Business and Management), bonded over their shared love of floorball, and decided to channel their frustration with corporate life into creating meaningful spaces.

text.text:
This coffee house is a hidden gem. Serving the finest blends of coffee and also some excellent food.
The atmosphere is calm and relaxed, and the location, although a little unusual, is like an oasis in amongst the city blocks.
There's an excellent selection of hot drinks, tea, coffee and cocoa, but you must also sample the food.
We went here for breakfast twice during our stay. The food is excellent, well presented and prepared with love and skill. The eggs benedict will blow your mind! Not only is it a sculpture, a work of art, but the the flavours and texture will transport you to a very happy place.
As with most local restaurants in Prague, the prices are very reasonable.
Also, being in the heart of Andel, there are many places to visit and shop within a few minutes walk.
If you're visiting Prague then don't miss out on this experience.

text.text:
Honestly, a very chill and calm place to sit in Karlín, with a long-standing reputation and really kind service. But that’s not everything. Let’s say the coffee is very good quality and nicely prepared, I also tried the Turkish eggs.

If I compare the price, taste, and preparation, it’s not that great. The eggs don’t have a runny yolk, the seasoning is okay, and the yogurt is fine. The brioche, though, wasn’t anything special, I’d much rather have a good slice of buttered bread.

Considering the dish costs over 200 CZK and the main task is to cook decent eggs (which isn’t exactly rocket science), it’s just not worth it.

It’s a pity, sending greetings to the kitchen, hoping they’ll put a bit more effort into it next time. I probably won’t order food there again, but the espresso tonic was just fine.

text.text:
Smaller but modern club in the center of Prague.

The concert we attended had really good sound sometimes a bit too strong, but overall fine. You can pay by card everywhere, except at the cloakroom. That’s probably why it was half empty. I doubt every foreign visitor carries 50 CZK in cash.

Please add a card terminal there to improve comfort people shouldn’t have to hold their things during the concert, especially when the ventilation is weak and it gets around 30 °C inside during the show. It’s definitely not comfortable to stand under the stage for a three-hour concert with clothes in your hands. It really affects the experience.

Also, they refused to take three items from me (a bag, jacket, and sweater). Apparently, I would have to pay twice and hang them separately. No thanks. In other countries (outside CZ and SK), I’ve never had this problem you can usually pay by card everywhere. And during winter, it was never an issue to hang three items on one hanger elsewhere.

Unfortunately, after this experience, I won’t be coming back to Roxy next time.

text.text: This music venue is a big disappointment for me. Not because of the service: that's fine; the hall has a comfortable bar with a good selection of drinks, clean toilets, and a cloakroom. Visitors feel safe in the venue. The problem is that they sold tickets well above the capacity of the hall, so the concert experience was terrible—you're practically standing in the hallway, under the balcony, with poor sound and an even worse view. I don't understand this strategy, because they could have raised the ticket price. Since they sold too many tickets, another problem arose at the entrance: people stood in line for at least 20 minutes, and the line ended only after an hour, when the opening act was already finishing. For me, this is my last visit (10/22/2025).

text.text: A must visit if you want to have a night out in Prague. Whether it’s for clubbing or just having a drink in an atypical place, this is the one. They have two stages (there was psytrance upstairs and minimal techno downstairs on that night), and an outdoor place. We went there on a Saturday night and even though the stage upstairs was packed, downstairs was pretty calm. The decor is what makes it really different from the other clubs/bar. Keep in mind that they only accept cash in CZK or euro (the exchange rate was 22). We loved it for it’s decor, but also because it’s not only a place for clubbing, you can very well just visit to have a drink, they have plenty of sitting place! The entrance was free on Sunday but for Saturday night it was 400 CZK. Prices are not cheap but ok, around 80 CZK for one cider and 60 CZK for a beer.

text.text:
Best ‘free entry’ night out I’ve ever had!

My friends and I went here on Jan 30th, as a warm up before the big New Years’ party. We arrived a bit past midnight. It was free entry, but not too many people, more on the empty size. Regardless, we had a great night.

ATMOSPHERE — the decor here steals the show, with its insane steampunk art! There is an upper and lower dancefloor, as well as a chill zone/bar with separate booths. The overall vibe is somewhere between mainstream and alt/underground. Bathrooms were clean!!

MUSIC — we went on a D&B night, DJs were OK. The basement was playing some very monotonous techno however, we are all techno fans but it was pretty boring

OVERALL — this is a really fun club, especially for free entry! Even better, there were plenty of places to hang our jackets, so it’s a great place if you don’t want to break the bank for a night out. I was so pleased by the price/value ratio I happily bought a cocktail (which I never do) to support the place. The music was kinda average and the club was surprisingly empty, but with a group of 5 we had a great time.

I swear the club will be fully open for real !

text.text: The worst nightclub that I have done. Students, in Prague for 3 days we hear a lot about the 5 floor of the nightclub so we decide to go on Wednesday. First it was free for students this night but we have the information only when we buy the tickets, 11 euros. We think it's okey for a place like this it's fine because it's written on our ticket that the tickets allowed us in the different rooms of the club, the ice bar and the robot one. What was our surprise when we arrived and learn that we need to pay an extra for the ice bar. Please do not put it on the ticket if we need to pay it. Furthermore, we start our tour in the night club when we discover that only two room are open this night. that was not say too in our tickets. As we can say it's a huge scam for tourists, quite expensive for two rooms. We were really disappointed. In addition to that, they give to our friends who didn't pay the ticket because they were students as us a free fortune wheel tickets 'cause they are students and refuse to give us it even if we are our students card due to the fact that we buy tickets without knowing that was free for us.
